allow:
Bash(cat > /project/courseData/aws-security-specialty/questions/aws-security-specialty.3.2.json << 'EOF'
[
  {"syllabus_id": "aws-security-specialty.3.2", "difficulty": 1, "question_type": "singleChoice", "question_text": "Which AWS service protects web applications from common attacks like SQL injection and XSS?", "options": {"a": "Network ACLs", "b": "AWS WAF \(Web Application Firewall\)", "c": "Security Groups", "d": "AWS Shield"}, "answer": "b", "tags": ["phase:atomic", "waf"]},
  {"syllabus_id": "aws-security-specialty.3.2", "difficulty": 2, "question_type": "singleChoice", "question_text": "How does AWS WAF Bot Control help protect login pages?", "options": {"a": "It encrypts credentials", "b": "It identifies and blocks automated bots attempting credential stuffing attacks", "c": "It forces password resets", "d": "It is only for blocking DDoS"}, "answer": "b", "tags": ["phase:complex", "bot-control"]},
  {"syllabus_id": "aws-security-specialty.3.2", "difficulty": 2, "question_type": "multiChoice", "question_text": "Which are components of a layered DDoS defense strategy? \(Select two\)", "options": {"a": "AWS Shield \(provides DDoS protection\)", "b": "AWS WAF \(filters attack traffic\)", "c": "Security groups \(prevent DDoS\)", "d": "AWS Firewall Manager \(central WAF management\)"}, "answer": "ab", "tags": ["phase:complex", "defense"]},
  {"syllabus_id": "aws-security-specialty.3.2", "difficulty": 3, "question_type": "singleChoice", "question_text": "Your WAF rules are generating false positives \(blocking legitimate traffic\). What is the best approach?", "options": {"a": "Disable WAF", "b": "Review logs, identify legitimate patterns, create exceptions \(IP allowlist\), adjust rate thresholds, and redeploy", "c": "Switch to Shield only", "d": "Implement no rules at all"}, "answer": "b", "tags": ["phase:complex", "tuning"]},
  {"syllabus_id": "aws-security-specialty.3.2", "difficulty": 2, "question_type": "freeText", "question_text": "Describe how to implement centralized WAF policy management across multiple AWS accounts using Firewall Manager.", "options": {}, "answer": "Process: \(1\) Central admin account creates Firewall Manager policy \(e.g., 'Block OWASP Top 10'\); \(2\) Policy includes managed rules \(Core Rule Set, Bot Control\); \(3\) Designate delegated administrator in member accounts; \(4\) Firewall Manager automatically deploys policy to ALBs/CloudFront/API Gateways in all member accounts; \(5\) Member accounts cannot override policy \(enforcement\); \(6\) Updates to policy propagate to all accounts automatically; \(7\) Logging: WAF logs from all accounts centralized \(S3 bucket accessible by admin account\); \(8\) Compliance dashboard shows: all resources protected with same rules, no gaps. Result: consistent security policy organization-wide, reduced administrative burden.", "tags": ["phase:integration", "management"]}
]
EOF)

Bash(cat > /project/courseData/aws-security-specialty/questions/aws-security-specialty.3.3.json << 'EOF'
[
  {"syllabus_id": "aws-security-specialty.3.3", "difficulty": 1, "question_type": "singleChoice", "question_text": "What is the purpose of Origin Access Control \(OAC\) in CloudFront?", "options": {"a": "Encrypt content at CDN edge", "b": "Restrict S3 access to only CloudFront \(prevent direct S3 URL access\)", "c": "Cache content globally", "d": "Add security headers"}, "answer": "b", "tags": ["phase:atomic", "oac"]},
  {"syllabus_id": "aws-security-specialty.3.3", "difficulty": 2, "question_type": "singleChoice", "question_text": "How can you enforce HTTPS and add security headers for all users accessing content through CloudFront?", "options": {"a": "Configure origin to require HTTPS only", "b": "Use Lambda@Edge to inject Strict-Transport-Security and X-Frame-Options headers at every request", "c": "Configure S3 bucket policy", "d": "Use Security Groups"}, "answer": "b", "tags": ["phase:complex", "headers"]},
  {"syllabus_id": "aws-security-specialty.3.3", "difficulty": 2, "question_type": "multiChoice", "question_text": "Which are benefits of using CloudFront Origin Shield? \(Select two\)", "options": {"a": "Protects origin from traffic spikes by absorbing load", "b": "Improves cache hit ratio", "c": "Eliminates need for WAF", "d": "Reduces costs for DDoS-prone origins"}, "answer": "ab", "tags": ["phase:complex", "origin-shield"]},
  {"syllabus_id": "aws-security-specialty.3.3", "difficulty": 3, "question_type": "singleChoice", "question_text": "Your CloudFront distribution should restrict access to authenticated users only. Which approach is most appropriate?", "options": {"a": "Use signed URLs or signed cookies to allow only authenticated users to access content", "b": "Disable public access entirely", "c": "Use security groups \(not applicable to CloudFront\)", "d": "No restriction is possible with CloudFront"}, "answer": "a", "tags": ["phase:complex", "access-control"]},
  {"syllabus_id": "aws-security-specialty.3.3", "difficulty": 2, "question_type": "freeText", "question_text": "Design a secure CloudFront distribution architecture for a SaaS application that must protect payment data and enforce geographic restrictions.", "options": {}, "answer": "Architecture: \(1\) CloudFront distribution → Origin Access Control restricts S3 to CloudFront only; \(2\) WAF Web ACL: \(a\) geo-blocking rule denies access from restricted countries; \(b\) managed rules block OWASP Top 10; \(c\) rate-based rules limit 2000 req/min per IP; \(3\) Lambda@Edge: \(a\) inject security headers \(HSTS, CSP\); \(b\) validate user authentication token; \(c\) require valid JWT in Authorization header; \(4\) Field-level encryption: payment fields \(credit card, CVV\) encrypted at edge before reaching origin; \(5\) Signed URLs: short-lived URLs \(5 min\) prevent sharing/reuse; \(6\) Logging: CloudFront logs all requests \(allowed/blocked by WAF\) to S3; analyze for attacks; \(7\) Origin Shield: absorbs DDoS traffic before reaching origin. Result: payment data protected, fraudsters geo-blocked, attacks detected, origin shielded.", "tags": ["phase:integration", "saas"]}
]
EOF)

Bash(cat > /project/courseData/aws-security-specialty/questions/aws-security-specialty.3.4.json << 'EOF'
[
  {"syllabus_id": "aws-security-specialty.3.4", "difficulty": 1, "question_type": "singleChoice", "question_text": "What is AWS Network Firewall and at which layer does it operate?", "options": {"a": "Layer 2 \(data link\)", "b": "Layer 4 \(transport\) and Layer 7 \(application\)", "c": "Layer 3 \(network\) only", "d": "Layer 1 \(physical\)"}, "answer": "b", "tags": ["phase:atomic", "network-firewall"]},
  {"syllabus_id": "aws-security-specialty.3.4", "difficulty": 2, "question_type": "singleChoice", "question_text": "How does DNS Firewall help prevent malware C2 communication?", "options": {"a": "It blocks DNS queries to known malicious domains", "b": "It encrypts DNS traffic", "c": "It prevents DNS spoofing only", "d": "It has no security benefit"}, "answer": "a", "tags": ["phase:complex", "dns-firewall"]},
  {"syllabus_id": "aws-security-specialty.3.4", "difficulty": 2, "question_type": "multiChoice", "question_text": "Which statements about Network Firewall are correct? \(Select two\)", "options": {"a": "Network Firewall operates at the VPC level", "b": "Network Firewall is stateful by default", "c": "Network Firewall is only for internet-facing applications", "d": "Network Firewall can be centralized for organization-wide protection"}, "answer": "ad", "tags": ["phase:complex", "firewall"]},
  {"syllabus_id": "aws-security-specialty.3.4", "difficulty": 3, "question_type": "singleChoice", "question_text": "Your organization has 50 workload VPCs. You want centralized network filtering. What architecture is recommended?", "options": {"a": "Deploy Network Firewall in each VPC", "b": "Deploy Network Firewall in centralized firewall VPC, route all traffic through it via Transit Gateway", "c": "Use WAF for all filtering", "d": "Network Firewall cannot be centralized"}, "answer": "b", "tags": ["phase:complex", "centralization"]},
  {"syllabus_id": "aws-security-specialty.3.4", "difficulty": 2, "question_type": "freeText", "question_text": "Describe how to use Network Firewall and Firewall Manager together to enforce organization-wide network security policies.", "options": {}, "answer": "Process: \(1\) Central admin account defines Network Firewall policy in Firewall Manager: \(a\) stateful rules allow known-safe outbound domains; \(b\) DNS Firewall blocks malicious domains; \(c\) logging to CloudWatch; \(2\) Designate delegated administrator; \(3\) Policy deployed to all VPCs in all accounts automatically; \(4\) All egress traffic routes through centralized firewall VPC \(using Transit Gateway\); \(5\) Network Firewall inspects all outbound traffic: \(a\) block to blocked domains; \(b\) block to dangerous ports; \(c\) allow to known-safe; \(6\) Logging aggregated: firewall logs from all accounts/regions centralized in admin account; \(7\) Monitoring: Security Hub shows firewall compliance, any policy violations. Result: organization-wide network control, single policy location, consistent enforcement.", "tags": ["phase:integration", "firewall-manager"]}
]
EOF)
